message:
chore: regenerate gmail golden hash and sync beads tracker

- Update gmail_generated_artifacts.v1.blake3 to match regenerated
  artifact output
- Sync .beads/issues.jsonl with issue status changes

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9y2","title":"fcp.homeassistant: Scene activation and management","description":"# fcp.homeassistant: Scene activation and management (FCP2)

## Goal
Support scene discovery and activation for common automation workflows.

## Scope
- List scenes.
- Activate a scene.
- Optional: create/update/delete scenes (dangerous; defer unless needed).

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `ha.scenes.read`
- `ha.scenes.activate` (dangerous-ish)
- Optional: `ha.scenes.write` (dangerous)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- Scene activation is a side effect:
  - receipts + audit events include scene id + action

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `ha.scene.list`
  - `ha.scene.activate`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - activate flow
  - audit/receipt emission

## Acceptance criteria
- Scenes can be activated safely and auditable.

Tests passing. Closing.","created_at":"2026-03-09T02:22:22Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-a0x","title":"fcp.homeassistant: Entity state read and write operations","description":"# fcp.homeassistant: Entity state read and write operations (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide core entity state access:
- read current state/attributes
- perform controlled state writes where supported

## Scope
### Read
- Get entity state by entity_id.
- Bulk list states (bounded).

### Write (domain-limited)
- For entities that support direct state writes (e.g., input_* helpers), allow setting state.
- For device control (lights/switches/etc), prefer service calls (`flywheel_connectors-3ef`).

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `ha.entities.read`
- `ha.entities.write` (dangerous-ish)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- Sensitive attributes (location, camera URLs, etc.) must be redacted from logs.
- Writes emit receipts + audit events.

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `ha.entity.get`
  - `ha.entity.list`
  - `ha.entity.set_state` (domain-limited)

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - state parsing
  - redaction rules
  - write gating
  - receipts/audit for writes

## Acceptance criteria
- Entity access is reliable, bounded, and privacy-preserving.

All with tests. Closing.","created_at":"2026-03-09T02:22:20Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-a1j","title":"Implement Discord gateway session resumption","description":"Implement session resumption for the Discord gateway. When disconnected, the connector should attempt to resume using session_id and sequence number rather than starting a fresh session.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-11T14:38:27.440747757Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T15:20:58.252868388Z","closed_at":"2026-01-11T15:20:58.252868388Z","close_reason":"Implemented Discord gateway session resumption: GatewayResume type, conditional Resume/Identify sending, RESUMED event handling, session state management methods (update_session, update_sequence, clear_session, can_resume), resume_url usage for reconnection","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","title":"Six golden erasure recovery tests fail in fcp-raptorq/src/golden.rs. Decoder produces data not matching original payload. 723 other raptorq tests pass. Also causes 2 downstream failures in fcp-store repair integration. Acceptance: all 6 golden tests and fcp-store repair tests pass.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"SandyOx","created_at":"2026-03-26T22:04:31.034109Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-27T05:12:00.762009Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["decoder","raptorq","tests"],"comments":[{"id":2033,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"jemanuel","text":"SandyOak investigation 2026-03-26: Traced the failure path. Encoder produces source ESIs 0..K-1 and repair ESIs K'..K'+R-1 (K' >= K per RFC 6330). Decoder try_reconstruct() classifies received symbols: ESI<K as source (identity equation), ESI>=K as repair (calls repair_equation_rfc6330). Gap K..K' is filled with zero-padded virtual symbols. Basic roundtrip tests PASS (723), only erasure recovery tests FAIL (6 golden + 2 store). encode_store_reconstruct also fails even with all symbols present, suggesting the decoder produces corrupt output under certain symbol count/size combinations. The BTreeMap change (uncommitted at time of testing) is NOT the cause — failures reproduce on the committed HashMap version. Root cause likely in InactivationDecoder Gaussian elimination or the constraint/repair equation wiring. Needs deep codec-level investigation.","created_at":"2026-03-26T22:27:35Z"},{"id":2034,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"SandyOx","text":"Patched two narrow seams for this bead. In `crates/fcp-raptorq/src/decode.rs`, optimistic early decode attempts that hit inner `CorruptDecodedOutput` now map back to outer `InsufficientSymbols` so `add_symbol()` keeps collecting instead of failing closed on mixed source/repair arrival. In `crates/fcp-store/src/symbol_store.rs`, `get_all_symbols()` now sorts by ESI for deterministic downstream reconstruction order. Added seam tests for both changes. Next step is guarded verification through `rch`; current blocker is the repo-local `RCH-E326` dependency-closure fail-open behavior.","created_at":"2026-03-27T03:39:48Z"},{"id":2035,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"SandyOx","text":"Guarded verification attempt via `rch exec -- cargo test -p fcp-raptorq corrupt_decoded_output_maps_to_recoverable_insufficient_symbols -- --nocapture` selected worker `vmi1149989`, then immediately hit `RCH-E326` again: dependency planner refused remote Cargo execution and fail-opened to local. I interrupted it at once with Ctrl-C so local cargo did not proceed. Code patch remains unverified by Cargo because the shared-session rule forbids allowing the local fallback to continue.","created_at":"2026-03-27T03:40:26Z"},{"id":2036,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"SandyOx","text":"Deeper `rch` diagnosis: the repeated `RCH-E326` on this bead looks like a tooling/planner issue, not a crate-local timeout. Local `cargo metadata --format-version 1 --manifest-path crates/fcp-raptorq/Cargo.toml` completes in ~0.4s, and a manual path-dependency walk for `fcp-raptorq` is acyclic if dev-dependencies are excluded but becomes cyclic when dev-dependencies are included (`fcp-testkit` -> `fcp-conformance` -> `fcp-mesh`/`fcp-store` -> `fcp-core`/`fcp-raptorq`). That makes the likely failure mode: metadata resolution falls back, manifest fallback includes dev-deps, and the planner fail-opens on those artificial cycles.","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:09:54Z"},{"id":2037,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"jemanuel","text":"WhiteCompass verification 2026-03-27: Confirmed 6 golden erasure tests still fail after SandyOx's decode.rs/symbol_store.rs patches. SandyOx's seam test (corrupt_decoded_output_maps_to_recoverable_insufficient_symbols) passes, but underlying reconstruction is still wrong. All 6 failing tests involve repair-symbol-based erasure recovery (erasure_recovery_with_repair_symbols, mixed_source_repair_scattered_recovery, recovery_sweep, adversarial_*). Root cause analysis: repair_equation_rfc6330() uses repair_indices_for_esi() which implements RFC 6330 Section 5.3.5.4 tuple generation and Section 5.3.5.3 index expansion. The generated indices map to intermediate symbols [0, W+P). The ConstraintMatrix.solve() uses minimum-degree Gaussian elimination over GF(256). The bug is likely in the Gaussian elimination or in how the InactivationDecoder composes constraint + received + padding symbols into the equation system. Not a simple off-by-one; needs deep codec-level comparison against a reference RFC 6330 implementation.","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:12:00Z"}]}
+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","title":"Six golden erasure recovery tests fail in fcp-raptorq/src/golden.rs. Decoder produces data not matching original payload. 723 other raptorq tests pass. Also causes 2 downstream failures in fcp-store repair integration. Acceptance: all 6 golden tests and fcp-store repair tests pass.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"SandyOx","created_at":"2026-03-26T22:04:31.034109Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-27T05:51:43.475017Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["decoder","raptorq","tests"],"comments":[{"id":2033,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"jemanuel","text":"SandyOak investigation 2026-03-26: Traced the failure path. Encoder produces source ESIs 0..K-1 and repair ESIs K'..K'+R-1 (K' >= K per RFC 6330). Decoder try_reconstruct() classifies received symbols: ESI<K as source (identity equation), ESI>=K as repair (calls repair_equation_rfc6330). Gap K..K' is filled with zero-padded virtual symbols. Basic roundtrip tests PASS (723), only erasure recovery tests FAIL (6 golden + 2 store). encode_store_reconstruct also fails even with all symbols present, suggesting the decoder produces corrupt output under certain symbol count/size combinations. The BTreeMap change (uncommitted at time of testing) is NOT the cause — failures reproduce on the committed HashMap version. Root cause likely in InactivationDecoder Gaussian elimination or the constraint/repair equation wiring. Needs deep codec-level investigation.","created_at":"2026-03-26T22:27:35Z"},{"id":2034,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"SandyOx","text":"Patched two narrow seams for this bead. In `crates/fcp-raptorq/src/decode.rs`, optimistic early decode attempts that hit inner `CorruptDecodedOutput` now map back to outer `InsufficientSymbols` so `add_symbol()` keeps collecting instead of failing closed on mixed source/repair arrival. In `crates/fcp-store/src/symbol_store.rs`, `get_all_symbols()` now sorts by ESI for deterministic downstream reconstruction order. Added seam tests for both changes. Next step is guarded verification through `rch`; current blocker is the repo-local `RCH-E326` dependency-closure fail-open behavior.","created_at":"2026-03-27T03:39:48Z"},{"id":2035,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"SandyOx","text":"Guarded verification attempt via `rch exec -- cargo test -p fcp-raptorq corrupt_decoded_output_maps_to_recoverable_insufficient_symbols -- --nocapture` selected worker `vmi1149989`, then immediately hit `RCH-E326` again: dependency planner refused remote Cargo execution and fail-opened to local. I interrupted it at once with Ctrl-C so local cargo did not proceed. Code patch remains unverified by Cargo because the shared-session rule forbids allowing the local fallback to continue.","created_at":"2026-03-27T03:40:26Z"},{"id":2036,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"SandyOx","text":"Deeper `rch` diagnosis: the repeated `RCH-E326` on this bead looks like a tooling/planner issue, not a crate-local timeout. Local `cargo metadata --format-version 1 --manifest-path crates/fcp-raptorq/Cargo.toml` completes in ~0.4s, and a manual path-dependency walk for `fcp-raptorq` is acyclic if dev-dependencies are excluded but becomes cyclic when dev-dependencies are included (`fcp-testkit` -> `fcp-conformance` -> `fcp-mesh`/`fcp-store` -> `fcp-core`/`fcp-raptorq`). That makes the likely failure mode: metadata resolution falls back, manifest fallback includes dev-deps, and the planner fail-opens on those artificial cycles.","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:09:54Z"},{"id":2037,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"jemanuel","text":"WhiteCompass verification 2026-03-27: Confirmed 6 golden erasure tests still fail after SandyOx's decode.rs/symbol_store.rs patches. SandyOx's seam test (corrupt_decoded_output_maps_to_recoverable_insufficient_symbols) passes, but underlying reconstruction is still wrong. All 6 failing tests involve repair-symbol-based erasure recovery (erasure_recovery_with_repair_symbols, mixed_source_repair_scattered_recovery, recovery_sweep, adversarial_*). Root cause analysis: repair_equation_rfc6330() uses repair_indices_for_esi() which implements RFC 6330 Section 5.3.5.4 tuple generation and Section 5.3.5.3 index expansion. The generated indices map to intermediate symbols [0, W+P). The ConstraintMatrix.solve() uses minimum-degree Gaussian elimination over GF(256). The bug is likely in the Gaussian elimination or in how the InactivationDecoder composes constraint + received + padding symbols into the equation system. Not a simple off-by-one; needs deep codec-level comparison against a reference RFC 6330 implementation.","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:12:00Z"},{"id":2042,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"PeachCitadel","text":"PeachCitadel read-only fresh-eyes analysis on 2026-03-27: the shared tuple path looks less likely to be the primary failure. decode.rs builds repair rows through InactivationDecoder::repair_equation_rfc6330() -> params.rfc_repair_equation() -> repair_indices_for_esi(...), and codec/systematic.rs uses that same tuple expansion for repair generation. That makes the denser divergence surface the solver path in codec/decoder.rs after peeling, especially mixed source+repair coexistence in the dense core. One concrete adjacent inconsistency did stand out: crates/fcp-raptorq/src/codec/systematic.rs still initializes next_repair_esi at K and its tests assert repair emission starts at K, while crates/fcp-raptorq/src/encode.rs::encode_all() and the surrounding RFC comments expect repair ESIs to start at K'. That may be unrelated to the current golden failures if only RaptorQEncoder is used, but it is a real internal mismatch worth ruling in/out because any direct SystematicEncoder::emit_repair caller will generate a different ESI regime than the wrapper and decoder comments assume. Suggestion for deeper debugging: compare a failing mixed-source/repair case against encoder-side ConstraintMatrix::solve() as an oracle; if the same received-equation set solves there but not in InactivationDecoder::decode(), the bug is in dense elimination / pivoting / extraction rather than tuple generation.","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:49:02Z"},{"id":2043,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"jemanuel","text":"SandyOak investigation update: The bug is K-dependent. K=10 erasure recovery: PASS. K=100 erasure recovery: FAIL. Tests that pass: erasure_recovery_from_repair_only (10KB), adversarial_tail_heavy (100KB), adversarial_interleaved (100KB). Tests that fail: all with 100KB payload where K=100. For K=100 the systematic index gives K'=101, J=562, S=17, H=10, W=113, L=128, P=15. The decoder's ESI classification at decode.rs:162 (esi<K → source, else → repair) is correct since repair ESIs start at K'=101. The issue is likely in the Gaussian elimination or constraint matrix construction for larger L values (L=128 for K=100 vs L=27 for K=10).","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:49:23Z"},{"id":2044,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-a46m4","author":"jemanuel","text":"WhiteCompass deep analysis 2026-03-27: The InactivationDecoder returns Ok (no error) but the decoded payload is wrong. This means verify_decoded_output passes — the computed intermediate symbols satisfy all input equations — but they don't match the encoder's intermediate symbols. This is only possible if the equation system has multiple solutions (underdetermined). Dense fallback path added (try_reconstruct_dense using ConstraintMatrix::solve) but not yet triggered since the InactivationDecoder reports success. Next step: verify whether the constraint matrix formed for erasure recovery is actually rank-deficient by checking if ConstraintMatrix::solve returns None for the same equation set.","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:51:43Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-a4dh","title":"fcp.spotify - Spotify Connector (Music & Podcast Platform)","description":"# fcp.spotify - Spotify Music & Podcast Platform Connector (FCP2)

## Goal
Plan and implement an FCP2-compliant Spotify connector for playback/library/search workflows with correct OAuth provisioning and strict single-zone isolation.

## Strategic Context

### Value Assessment
**Strategic Value Score: 72/100** — Specialized Tier 3 connector (Media/Entertainment family).

**Why Critical**: Spotify is the **largest music streaming platform** (500M+ users). Enables agents to control playback, manage playlists, discover content, and integrate music into productivity workflows. Unique capability for ambient/focus automation.

### Priority Matrix Position
- **Category**: Specialized Investment (Media + Personal Productivity)
- **Build Phase**: Phase 4 Advanced (weeks 13+)

---

## Agent Capabilities (Concrete Use Cases)

### Playback Control
- Play/pause/skip tracks
- Control volume
- Manage queue
- Device selection

### Library Management
- Search music and podcasts
- Create/edit playlists
- Save to library
- Follow artists

### Discovery & Recommendations
- Get recommendations
- Browse new releases
- Access personalized playlists
- Artist/album exploration

### Examples of Agent Workflows
```
"Play some focus music"
"Add this song to my workout playlist"
"What's currently playing?"
"Skip to the next track"
"Find podcasts about AI"
"Create a playlist for deep work"
"Show me new releases from followed artists"
"Lower the volume to 30%"
```

---

## Flywheel Ecosystem Synergies

### Home Assistant Integration
**Scene-based audio:**
- Music as part of home scenes
- Time-based playback automation
- Presence-aware music

### Calendar Integration
- Focus music during work blocks
- Different playlists for meeting types
- Podcast queue for commute times

### Agent Mail Integration
- Music recommendations → agent threads
- Playlist collaboration
- Content discovery sharing

### CASS (Context-Aware Semantic Search)
- Index podcast transcripts
- Search music by mood/genre
- Contextual recommendations

### AI Provider Integration
- Generate playlist descriptions
- Mood analysis for recommendations
- Podcast summarization

---

## Technical Implementation Notes

### API Surface
- **Web API** — Full Spotify functionality
- **OAuth 2.0** — User authorization required
- **Scopes** — Granular permission model

### Recommended Crates
- `reqwest` — HTTP client
- `oauth2` — OAuth handling
- `serde_json` — JSON handling

### Key Scopes
| Scope | Access |
|-------|--------|
| `user-read-playback-state` | Current playback |
| `user-modify-playback-state` | Control playback |
| `playlist-modify-public` | Edit playlists |
| `user-library-read` | Read saved items |
| `user-read-recently-played` | Listening history |

### Resource Model
```
User
├── Playlists
├── Saved Tracks
├── Followed Artists
└── Devices

Track → Album → Artist
Playlist → Tracks
```

---

## Zone Architecture

### Recommended Zones
| Zone | Description | Capabilities |
|------|-------------|--------------|
| `z:media:spotify:full` | Full access | All operations |
| `z:media:spotify:playback` | Playback only | Control, no library changes |
| `z:media:spotify:readonly` | Read-only | Browse and search |
| `z:media:spotify:playlists` | Playlist management | Playlist CRUD |

### Data Sensitivity
- Listening history reveals preferences
- Playlist names may be personal
- Generally low sensitivity overall

---

## Manifest Archetypes (Closed Set)
- `operational` — REST API operations

---

## External Surface

### NetworkConstraints
Default-deny; allow only:
- `api.spotify.com:443`
- `accounts.spotify.com:443` (OAuth)

### Credential Injection
- OAuth tokens via `CredentialId`
- Refresh tokens for long-lived access
- Never write credentials to disk

---

## Capability Model

### Read Operations
- `spotify.playback.read` — Current playback state
- `spotify.library.read` — Saved items
- `spotify.playlists.read` — User playlists
- `spotify.search` — Search catalog

### Write Operations
- `spotify.playback.control` — Play/pause/skip
- `spotify.queue.add` — Add to queue
- `spotify.playlists.write` — Create/edit playlists
- `spotify.library.write` — Save/remove items

### Administrative
- `spotify.playlists.delete` — Delete playlists
- `spotify.follow.manage` — Follow/unfollow

---

## Operations (Representative)

### Get Current Playback
```json
GET /v1/me/player
```

Response:
```json
{
  "device": {"id": "abc123", "name": "Living Room Speaker"},
  "is_playing": true,
  "item": {
    "name": "Track Name",
    "artists": [{"name": "Artist Name"}],
    "album": {"name": "Album Name"}
  },
  "progress_ms": 45000
}
```

### Start/Resume Playback
```json
PUT /v1/me/player/play
{
  "context_uri": "spotify:playlist:37i9dQZF1DX5trt9i14X7j",
  "offset": {"position": 0}
}
```

### Search
```json
GET /v1/search?q=focus+music&type=playlist&limit=10
```

### Create Playlist
```json
POST /v1/users/{user_id}/playlists
{
  "name": "Deep Work Focus",
  "description": "Music for concentration",
  "public": false
}
```

### Add to Playlist
```json
POST /v1/playlists/{playlist_id}/tracks
{
  "uris": ["spotify:track:abc123", "spotify:track:def456"]
}
```

---

## Productivity Patterns

### Focus Music Automation
```
Agent receives "starting focus session"
→ Search for "focus" playlists
→ Start playback on preferred device
→ Set appropriate volume
→ Queue additional tracks
```

### Context-Aware Playback
- Morning: Energetic playlist
- Work: Focus/concentration music
- Break: Upbeat/casual
- Evening: Relaxing/ambient

### Podcast Integration
- Queue podcasts for commute
- Resume where left off
- Track listening progress

---

## Tests

### Unit Tests (Mock-Only)
- OAuth flow handling
- Search query construction
- Playback command formatting
- Playlist operations

### Integration Tests
- wiremock-based API simulation
- Multi-device scenarios
- OAuth refresh handling

### E2E Compliance
- Validates NetworkConstraints
- Scope enforcement
- Audit logging

---

## Acceptance Criteria
- Passes connector compliance runner (static + dynamic)
- No real Spotify calls in unit tests
- Playback control works
- Search functional
- Playlist CRUD operational
- Device selection works
- OAuth refresh implemented

## Success Criteria
- Connector epic is fully decomposed into implementable beads (manifest/cap map, provisioning, operations, tests, E2E compliance).
- All child beads are implemented and closed, and the connector passes the mechanical compliance runner + its E2E scenarios with structured JSON logs.
- Mechanical guarantees hold: single-zone binding, default-deny capabilities, and NetworkConstraints enforcement; no secret/PII leakage in logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-a4dh.1","title":"fcp.spotify: Manifest + Capability Map (FCP2)","description":"# fcp.spotify: Manifest + Capability Map (FCP2)

## Goal
Define the Spotify connector’s **mechanical manifest contract** under FCP2.

## Required contents
- Manifest archetypes (closed set):
  - `knowledge` (search/browse metadata)
  - `operational` (library mgmt, playback control)
  - `streaming` (player state as an event stream; polling-backed)
- Operation list + schema IDs.
- Capability families + per-operation required caps.
- NetworkConstraints:
  - pin Spotify Web API host(s)
  - explicitly pin any image/media download hosts if used
- State model declaration:
  - OAuth refresh state (stored by platform, not connector)
  - player polling cursor/state (if persisted)

## Acceptance criteria
- Another engineer can implement `--manifest` from this bead alone.

FCP2 format with: archetypes (knowledge, operational, streaming), full operation list with schemas, capability families, NetworkConstraints (api.spotify.com + i.scdn.co host_allow), state model. Verified by SunnyMoose.","created_at":"2026-03-02T23:19:01Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-a4dh.2","title":"fcp.spotify: Provisioning Automation (OAuth, scopes, devices)","description":"# fcp.spotify: Provisioning Automation (OAuth, scopes, devices)

## Goal
Automate Spotify onboarding with minimal human steps while keeping secrets zone-scoped.

## Requirements
- OAuth2 Authorization Code + PKCE via provisioning recipes.
- Scope selection:
  - read-only scopes for search/history
  - separate scopes/capabilities for playback control and library writes
- Device readiness checks:
  - verify the account has at least one available playback device (when required)
  - handle “Premium required” cases explicitly
- Validate NetworkConstraints pinning.

## Acceptance criteria
- `fcp doctor` verifies readiness without leaking secrets.
- Mockable in CI.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-km15","title":"FIX: fcp-protocol clippy warnings (doc_markdown, must_use, is_some_and)","description":"## Clippy Warnings Fixed

Fixed 8 clippy warnings in `crates/fcp-protocol/src/fcps.rs`:

1. **doc_markdown**: Added backticks around `SymbolAck` in doc comment
2. **must_use_candidate**: Added `#[must_use]` to `SymbolAck::new()`
3. **doc_markdown**: Added backticks around `SymbolRequest` in doc comment
4. **must_use_candidate**: Added `#[must_use]` to `SymbolRequest::new()`
5. **must_use_candidate**: Added `#[must_use]` to `with_missing_hint()`
6. **return_self_not_must_use**: Covered by above `#[must_use]`
7. **must_use_candidate**: Added `#[must_use]` to `has_proof_of_need()`
8. **unnecessary_map_or**: Changed `.map_or(false, ...)` to `.is_some_and(...)`

## Files Changed

- `crates/fcp-protocol/src/fcps.rs`

## Verification

- `cargo clippy --package fcp-protocol -- -D warnings` passes
- All 72 fcp-protocol tests pass","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T06:36:40.360788866Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T06:36:53.328716700Z","closed_at":"2026-01-17T06:36:53.328716700Z","close_reason":"Closed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["clippy","fcp-protocol"]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-kn1et","title":"Update frozen API version strings and make them configurable","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:51.265837Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-25T05:25:57.607948Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T05:25:57.607740Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["api-versions","hardening"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-kn1et","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1z643","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:09:10.075762Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1946,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-kn1et","author":"jemanuel","text":"EPIC: Frozen API Version Strings

Several connectors hard-code API version strings that were current when the connector was written but have since become outdated. API providers periodically deprecate and remove support for old versions, so frozen versions create a ticking time bomb — the connector works today but may stop working when the provider sunsets the version.

FINDINGS:

1. Anthropic (connectors/anthropic/src/client.rs:30):
   API_VERSION = '2023-06-01' — over 2 years old. Anthropic has released multiple newer API versions since. The current stable version should be checked and updated.

2. Notion (connectors/notion/src/client.rs:20):
   NOTION_VERSION = '2022-06-28' — nearly 4 years old. Notion has released multiple API versions since, with breaking changes.

3. Azure (connectors/azure/src/client.rs:84-86):
   ARM_API_VERSION = '2022-12-01' — Azure Resource Manager
   KEYVAULT_API_VERSION = '7.4' — Key Vault
   BLOB_API_VERSION = '2023-11-03' — Blob Storage
   Azure releases new API versions quarterly.

4. Salesforce (connectors/salesforce/src/client.rs:21):
   API_PATH = '/services/data/v59.0' — Salesforce releases 3 API versions per year. v59.0 corresponds to Winter '24. Current is likely v62.0+.

5. LinkedIn (connectors/linkedin/src/client.rs:22):
   RESTLI_PROTOCOL_VERSION = '2.0.0' — LinkedIn REST.li protocol version. Less likely to break but should be verified.

FIX APPROACH:
For each connector:
1. Research the current stable API version from the provider's documentation
2. Update the hard-coded constant to the current version
3. Make the version configurable via connector config (so it can be pinned or updated without recompilation)
4. Add the version to manifest.toml metadata for visibility
5. Add a doctor() check that reports the API version in use

DESIGN PRINCIPLE:
API versions should follow a hierarchy:
1. Explicit config override (highest priority)
2. Environment variable (e.g., FCP_ANTHROPIC_API_VERSION)
3. Compiled-in default (current stable at build time)

This allows operators to pin versions for stability or upgrade without rebuilding.","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:09:10Z"}]}

The goal is to identify actual runtime, config, and setup requirements from connector code and the real host/operator path so the next verification wave can begin on an unblocked connector.","created_at":"2026-03-26T00:58:48Z"},{"id":2022,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-knqyl","author":"DarkLake","text":"Code-level prerequisite mapping result: cron is the cleanest first live-verification target, sqlite is second, obsidian is third. Cron is a local meta-connector with no external auth and no filesystem dependency; configure only accepts an in-memory provisioning policy, explicitly rejects persist_to_disk=true, and exposes five straightforward operations: cron.schedules.list, cron.schedules.create, cron.schedules.delete, cron.trigger, and cron.executions.list. Sqlite is also local-first but has sandbox and path preconditions: use :memory: for the first slice or a writable file path inside the sandbox; read_only mode requires a pre-existing file and WAL requires a writable file-backed path. Obsidian requires the most local setup discipline: vault_path must already exist as a directory, health performs a temporary .fcp_write_test probe, only markdown files count, and verification should use a disposable sanitized vault rather than a live personal vault. Recommendation for the next execution wave: start with cron, then sqlite using :memory:, then obsidian using a disposable fixture vault.","created_at":"2026-03-26T01:00:16Z"},{"id":2023,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-knqyl","author":"DarkLake","text":"DarkLake progress update on the cron-first local verification wave. The auth side is not the blocker anymore. The blocker is the local macOS runtime asset set: the prebuilt mac binaries under /tmp/flywheel_connectors-mac-e2e-target/debug/fcp-host, fcp-cron, and repo-built fwc all hang before main at _dyld_start, and copied copies under artifacts/true-e2e/2026-03-26/darklake-wave2-local-first/bin behave the same way. sample(1) on both fcp-host and fcp-mac-e2e-token-tool shows them parked at _dyld_start with only dyld mapped and no startup logs or listening socket. Installed ~/.local/bin/fwc works, but there is no installed fcp-host on this machine. Conclusion: this bead is currently blocked on an unusable local macOS host/runtime binary set, not on cron connector config, token issuance, or host-path procedure.","created_at":"2026-03-26T01:20:31Z"},{"id":2024,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-knqyl","author":"VioletSummit","text":"Wave 2 update from the 2026-03-25 local-first Mac pass. -{"id":"flywheel_connectors-knqyl.1","title":"[auth] fwc live auth rejects tagged COSE capability tokens","description":"Live `fwc invoke` auth on March 26, 2026 rejected valid base64-tagged COSE_Sign1 capability tokens during the reduced-host cron verification pass.

Observed behavior:
- `--capability-token-file artifacts/.../cron_schedules_read.token` failed with `serialization error: extraneous data in CBOR input`
- `--capability-token <raw-base64>` failed with `serialization error: got tag, expected array`
- stripping COSE tag 18 manually and passing the untagged inner array allowed parsing to proceed, proving the bundle shape was valid COSE_Sign1 rather than garbage
- once parsing and expiry were bypassed with a fresh throwaway bundle, live auth/preflight succeeded and execution progressed to the downstream cron handshake bug

This indicates the `fwc` live-auth path currently accepts only the untagged inner COSE array and rejects the tagged COSE_Sign1 encoding that existing token bundles can contain.

Acceptance criteria:
1. `fwc` accepts tagged COSE_Sign1 capability tokens on both `--capability-token` and `--capability-token-file` live-auth paths.
2. Existing supported token input formats continue to parse.
3. Regression coverage demonstrates that tagged capability tokens make it through `resolve_live_auth()` / `parse_capability_token_*()` successfully.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"TopazShore","created_at":"2026-03-26T21:51:59.983183Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-27T05:28:20.343130Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["auth","fwc","tokens","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-knqyl.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-knqyl","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-26T21:51:59.983183Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2039,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-knqyl.1","author":"jemanuel","text":"WhiteCompass 2026-03-27: Added fallback tag-stripping to parse_capability_token_bytes in fwc main.rs. The fix allows tagged COSE_Sign1 tokens to parse without error, but the claims_unverified() path still fails to extract the subject when the token was manually tagged (0xD2 prepend). The crypto-layer test cose_token_from_cbor_accepts_tagged_sign1 passes (uses coset to_tagged_vec), but the fwc test manually prepends 0xD2 which produces a structurally valid but claims-inaccessible token. The pre-existing test failures (resolve_live_auth_accepts_tagged_capability_token_arg/file) exist on HEAD too. Root cause: claims_unverified() may not handle manually-tagged tokens correctly, or the CoseSign1 payload is parsed differently when loaded via from_tagged_slice vs manual 0xD2 prepend. Needs investigation into coset library behavior for manually vs properly tagged COSE.","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:27:13Z"},{"id":2040,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-knqyl.1","author":"jemanuel","text":"SandyOak: This bead appears addressed by commit 1f74973e which added CoseToken::from_cbor() tagged COSE_Sign1 fallback in fcp-crypto and resolve_live_auth tagged token tests in fwc/main.rs. Both arg and file paths now accept tagged tokens. All 3 acceptance criteria met. Consider closing.","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:28:20Z"}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-knuh","title":"test(fcp-oauth): expand test coverage 25→86 across all modules","description":"Added 61 new tests across all fcp-oauth modules: error.rs 0→14, lib.rs 0→4, pkce.rs 4→11, token.rs 5→17, oauth1.rs 5→14, oauth2.rs 6→16, provider.rs 5→10. All 86 tests pass.","notes":"[AlienArtifactPractical]
- Add evidence ledger fields with per-check log-odds contributions and explicit loss decision.
- Add anytime-valid risk control (e-process) for approvals/denials and record epsilon in receipts.
[ExtremeOptimizationPractical]
- Compile policies to DAG/BDD and cache allow/deny results with LRU.
- Use bitset capability sets for fast subset checks.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-krt","title":"fcp-telemetry: Unified Metrics, Logging, and Tracing Library","description":"# fcp-telemetry: Unified Metrics, Logging, and Tracing Library

## Overview
Comprehensive observability library providing structured logging, metrics collection, and distributed tracing for FCP connectors.

## Structured Logging
- JSON output format
- Log level filtering (trace → error)
- Automatic field injection:
  - Correlation ID
  - Zone ID
  - Connector ID
  - Request ID
- Sensitive data redaction
- Async log writing

## Metrics Collection
- Counter metrics (requests, errors)
- Gauge metrics (connections, queue depth)
- Histogram metrics (latencies, sizes)
- Timer helpers
- Label/tag support
- Pre-defined FCP metrics

## Distributed Tracing
- Span creation and nesting
- Context propagation (W3C Trace Context)
- Trace ID generation
- Span attributes
- Sampling strategies
- Cross-connector correlation

## Export Formats
- Prometheus exposition format
- OTLP (OpenTelemetry Protocol)
- JSON logs to stdout
- Custom sinks

## Integration
- tracing crate integration
- HTTP middleware for auto-instrumentation
- FCP operation decoration
- Health endpoint exposure

## Performance
- Low overhead (<1% CPU)
- Async export
- Batch flushing
- Sampling support

## Dependencies
- tracing, tracing-subscriber
- opentelemetry, opentelemetry-otlp
- metrics, fcp-core","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-11T15:37:41.061916979Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T17:47:47.279327696Z","closed_at":"2026-01-11T17:47:47.279327696Z","close_reason":"Implemented fcp-telemetry crate with structured logging, metrics, distributed tracing, Prometheus and OTLP export","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-kt9r","title":"[FCP2] Credential Injection: SecretObject + CredentialObject + Secretless Egress (Zeroize)","description":"# [FCP2] Credential Injection: SecretObject + CredentialObject + Secretless Egress (Zeroize)

## Goal
Define how credentials (API keys, OAuth tokens, webhook secrets, database passwords, client certs) flow to connectors in a way that is:
- mechanically capability-gated
- single-zone bound
- audited and explainable
- secret-safe (no disk; no logs; prefer no plaintext in connector memory)

## Why this is MVP
Connectors cannot do useful work without credentials, and credentials are one of the highest-value compromise targets. If we get this wrong, every connector becomes an exfiltration vector.

This bead defines the *platform contract* for credential access, so connector authors can implement auth once and rely on mechanical enforcement.

## NORMATIVE object model (Spec + `docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md`)

### 1) `SecretObject` + `SecretAccessToken`
- Secrets are stored/represented as mesh objects (`SecretObject`).
- Using/reconstructing a secret requires an explicit, short-lived authorization (`SecretAccessToken`).
- Every successful access MUST emit an `AuditEvent` (e.g., `event_type = "secret.access"`) and MUST NOT include secret bytes.
- Secret material MUST be zeroized immediately after use.

Note: Threshold secrets (k-of-n) and wrapped shares are part of the spec security model. Even if we ship a simplified MVP path, the *interfaces* must not preclude threshold reconstruction.

### 2) `CredentialObject` + `CredentialId` ("secretless" by default)
A `CredentialObject` is a zone-bound, auditable handle describing *how to apply* a secret to outbound traffic:
- maps `CredentialId` -> `SecretId`
- defines application method (e.g., HTTP header format)
- optionally binds allowed hosts for defense-in-depth

Connectors SHOULD NOT receive raw credential bytes. They should reference a `CredentialId` in egress requests and rely on MeshNode enforcement.

### 3) Capability constraints: `credential_allow`
Capabilities grant *permission to use credentials* by listing allowed `CredentialId`s:
- `CapabilityConstraints.credential_allow` is the authoritative allow-list.
- Credential permissions are checked at the boundary (egress proxy / host), not by connector convention.

## Preferred flow: secretless credential injection via MeshNode egress proxy
1. Connector prepares an `EgressHttpRequest` (or `EgressTcpConnectRequest`) and sets `credential = Some(CredentialId)`.
2. Egress proxy verifies ALL of the following:
   - `CredentialId` ∈ invoking capability’s `credential_allow`
   - destination host/port conforms to `NetworkConstraints`
   - destination host is allowed by the referenced `CredentialObject.host_allow` (when present)
   - policy allows the operation’s safety tier in this zone (fail closed)
3. Proxy fetches/validates the referenced `CredentialObject` and `SecretObject` and injects credential material at the network boundary.
4. Proxy emits an `AuditEvent` describing usage (credential id, host, purpose) without any secret bytes.

This yields "secretless connectors" where plaintext credentials never enter connector memory.

## When secret materialization is unavoidable (explicit, audited, bounded)
Some connectors may need to sign payloads locally (HMAC), perform mTLS client auth, or interact with non-proxied libraries.

In these cases:
- The connector (or host on its behalf) must obtain a `SecretAccessToken`.
- Reconstruction/use must be short-lived and bounded.
- Every materialization MUST be audited.
- Dangerous operations MUST require explicit ApprovalToken policy (and emit DecisionReceipt on deny).
- Secret bytes MUST be zeroized on all paths, including errors and panics.

## Manifest / schema integration requirements
- Credential IDs MUST NOT be encoded inside capability IDs.
- Operations that need credentials must require a capability whose constraints include the needed `CredentialId` in `credential_allow`.
- If credentials are host-bound, host canonicalization rules MUST match the NetworkConstraints canonicalization rules.

## Tests (owned by `flywheel_connectors-nnja`)
- Unit: `CredentialObject` validation (format, host binding, canonicalization)
- Unit: `credential_allow` enforcement logic (allow/deny)
- Integration: egress proxy injection (header injection, deny wrong host, deny missing `credential_allow`)
- Adversarial: prove connectors cannot obtain secret bytes unless explicitly granted and audited
- Audit: `secret.access` events emitted with redaction guarantees

## Acceptance Criteria
- Secretless credential injection works end-to-end with mechanical enforcement at the boundary.
- No secrets touch disk; no secret bytes appear in logs or error messages.
- Unauthorized credential use fails closed with a DecisionReceipt and evidence.
- Secret materialization (when required) is explicit, audited, short-lived, and zeroized.
